Q1: I've always been someone who prefers structure, so I typically blocked out chunks of time on weekends and a few weekday evenings for studying. With 17 online courses to manage, I had to be strategic about it. I'd usually dedicate about 23 hours a week, spreading it across different subjects depending on what was due. I relied heavily on my planner and color-coded everything by course and priority level.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what was happening in my personal life. I tried to maintain some consistency, but honestly, with so many courses running simultaneously, I often had to shuffle things around. Some weeks I'd be deep into one subject, other weeks I'd barely touch it because another course demanded attention. The rhythm was more reactive than I would have liked.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new announcements or seeing if grades had been posted. I'd also log in when I knew an assignment was coming due or when I needed to download reading materials. Sometimes I'd get an email notification that prompted me to check in. It wasn't always about active studying—sometimes it was just administrative housekeeping.
Q4: For a while, logging in became almost automatic, especially in the mornings with my coffee. I'd do a quick scan of all my courses to see what needed attention that day. It became routine when I realized I couldn't afford to miss important updates across 17 courses. But I'll admit, the routine felt more like maintenance than genuine engagement sometimes.
Q5: The reading materials and written lecture notes were my lifeline—I'm very much a read-write learner. I'd download PDFs and articles and really engage with the text, highlighting and annotating everything. The discussion forums were also helpful because they forced me to articulate my understanding. Anything I could print or save for offline reading felt most valuable to me.
Q6: I often skipped the video lectures or only watched them at double speed. With over 100 minutes of video content, I just couldn't justify sitting through it all when reading was faster for me. Some of the interactive quizzes also felt like busywork rather than genuine learning opportunities. If something didn't feel directly connected to my grades or understanding, I'd deprioritize it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assessment outcomes or genuinely deepened my understanding of the material. I appreciated when I could see the practical application of what I was learning. If I finished something and felt like I'd actually grown intellectually, that was meaningful. Time is precious when you're juggling this many courses.
Q8: Optional activities often felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. I'd look at them and think, 'that would be nice,' but then reality would set in about all the required work still pending. Required activities had a sense of urgency that optional ones lacked. I did engage with some optional content when it genuinely interested me, but that was rare given my workload.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my real studying happened offline with printed materials, handwritten notes, and textbooks I'd borrowed from the library. I'd download everything I needed in one session and then work from my desk without being online. I also used external academic databases and journals that weren't part of the LMS. My most productive study sessions were completely disconnected from the internet.
Q10: There were countless times I'd spend hours with printed articles spread across my desk, making connections and writing notes, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd have deep study sessions at coffee shops with no wifi, working through problems on paper. Even my most intense exam preparation happened with physical flashcards and handwritten summaries. The LMS would show nothing for those days.
Q11: That was basically my standard approach. I'd log in, download everything available, and then study from those files for days or weeks without going back online. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could work without distractions. One download session might fuel a week of offline studying. It was more efficient for my learning style.
Q12: There were definitely periods when I struggled, particularly when I was feeling sad or when the stress became overwhelming. Even though I'm generally resilient, the sheer volume of 17 courses sometimes felt crushing. During those times, I'd still log in but I wasn't really absorbing anything. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped, but engagement was harder during those low periods.
Q13: Fatigue was a real issue—I'd sometimes log in late at night when I was exhausted and just scroll through without retaining anything. The competing responsibilities of managing so many courses meant something always had to give. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely because I didn't want to face it. Workload definitely impacted the quality of my engagement, not just the quantity.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have the LMS open while my mind was somewhere else entirely. I might be watching a video but thinking about a different assignment or personal matters. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph multiple times without it sinking in. Being logged in didn't mean I was truly present or learning.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts more than I should have, even though I knew they mattered for my participation grade. The sadness I was experiencing made it hard to muster the energy for things that required social interaction, even online. I also avoided some challenging readings when I was stressed, which wasn't smart but felt necessary for self-preservation. My assignment completion rate reflects those avoidance patterns.
Q16: Deadlines were essentially my external motivation system. I'd structure my entire week around what was due and when, often working backward from submission dates. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more. They created urgency that my internal motivation wasn't always providing, especially during lower periods.
Q17: Feedback was meaningful when it came, though I sometimes had to wait a while for it. Positive feedback energized me to engage more deeply with subsequent work. Critical feedback made me more careful but sometimes also more anxious about future submissions. I tried to use feedback constructively, but my response time to acting on it varied based on my emotional state.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework, but with moderate engagement requirements, I sometimes felt like I could coast more than I should. Clear modules and organized content helped me plan my offline studying. However, the structure didn't always account for how overwhelming it is to manage 17 courses simultaneously. I appreciated flexibility but sometimes needed more scaffolding.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement much easier for my learning style. What made it harder was when courses relied heavily on video content or required constant online presence. The discussion forums were well-designed and encouraged genuine peer interaction, which I valued. Courses that allowed asynchronous engagement fit my life better than those requiring real-time participation.
Q20: I think students might be searching for something, trying to understand what's expected, or feeling lost about where to focus their efforts. High activity could also mean they're anxious and checking obsessively without actually completing work. Sometimes you're in the LMS because you're trying to figure out what you're supposed to be doing rather than doing it. It might also reflect perfectionism—researching extensively before feeling ready to submit.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My exam scores and assignment quality were strong because I did extensive work offline that the LMS never captured. I could have low login frequency but be deeply engaged with printed materials for hours. Performance and LMS activity aren't the same thing, especially for students who prefer reading and writing over digital engagement. Quality of engagement matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, timing, and completion of specific tasks—the skeleton of engagement. But they miss the depth of understanding, the emotional investment, and all the learning that happens away from the screen. They can't capture whether someone was truly thinking critically or just going through motions. The logs show behavior, not cognition or genuine learning.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm intellectually invested and actively wrestling with the material, not just consuming it passively. It's when I find myself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, making connections to other knowledge. True engagement involves curiosity and a desire to understand deeply, not just to complete tasks. It's a mental and emotional state, not just a behavioral one.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed readings, the conversations I had with classmates outside the platform, or the genuine intellectual growth I experienced. It wouldn't capture the sadness I was working through or how the mindfulness interventions helped me cope. It also wouldn't show my resilience—how I kept going despite low motivation and high stress. The numbers tell a partial story at best.
